oneSentenceAnswer: Most leading AI labs now ship three sizes of model per family, a fast small one, a balanced mid one, and a deep flagship that can think before it answers.
fiveMinutePath: See the three-tier shape every major lab now ships.
Learn the jargon: tokens, context window, reasoning, multimodal.
Compare the named flagships head-to-head on a few real tasks.
Spot common myths about benchmarks and pricing.
Pick a model for your next project using a short checklist.
whyItMatters: Picking the wrong tier wastes money or breaks latency budgets.
Capabilities differ more than names suggest, especially on reasoning.
Public benchmarks lag real life; a methodology is part of the score.

keyIdeas.title: Every family ships three tiers
keyIdeas.body: Labs offer a small fast tier, a mid balanced tier, and a flagship for hard problems. Picking the tier matters more than picking the lab.

keyIdeas.title: Reasoning is its own product surface
keyIdeas.body: Some models can think before answering. They cost more and take longer, but they reduce silly mistakes on math, code, and planning.

keyIdeas.title: Context windows are getting absurd
keyIdeas.body: Modern flagships can read hundreds of thousands of tokens. Bigger is not always better; retrieval still beats stuffing a whole codebase.

keyIdeas.title: Multimodal is default, not optional
keyIdeas.body: Vision is built in across the major families. Audio in and out is shipping fast. You can hand a model a photo or a PDF and ask questions.

keyIdeas.title: Price falls, capability rises
keyIdeas.body: Inference cost per million tokens dropped roughly an order of magnitude in two years. The cheap tier today beats yesterday's flagship.

diagrams.alt: Concept map: one model family splits into Small (fast, cheap), Mid (balanced), and Flagship (reasoning, long context) tiers, each with its best use, separated by four axes — speed, cost, reasoning, context length.

diagrams.alt: Sequence diagram showing how a reasoning model thinks before answering
diagrams.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant M as Reasoning model
  U->>M: Question
  M-->>M: Plan steps (hidden)
  M-->>M: Verify each step
  M->>U: Final answer + (optional) summary of thought

timeline.title: Flagship sizes consolidate
timeline.body: Labs settle on a small/mid/large shape per family, dropping older one-size-fits-all lines.

timeline.title: Reasoning models go mainstream
timeline.body: A dedicated reasoning surface ships across two major labs, with separate pricing and latency profiles.

timeline.title: Context jumps past 1M tokens
timeline.body: Long-context flagships read hundreds of thousands of tokens in one call; agentic use cases get viable.

timeline.title: Per-token price crosses a cent floor
timeline.body: Mid-tier inference falls below one cent per thousand tokens for many providers.

cells.Strengths: Cheap and fast. Great for routing, classification, short answers.
Balanced. The right default for most product features.
cells.Watch out for: Slower than small; can still hallucinate on niche facts.

cells.Strengths: Long context, reasoning, planning, multimodal depth.
cells.Watch out for: 5-20x cost; latency in seconds; rate limits.

examples.title: Tag 10k customer emails by intent
examples.body: Use a small-tier model. Batch them. You want throughput, not deep reasoning.

examples.title: Review a 3-file pull request
examples.body: Use a mid-tier model with structured output. Ask for findings keyed by file and severity.

examples.title: Summarize a 200-page contract
examples.body: Use a flagship with long context. Add retrieval over the table of contents for citations.

analogies.setup: Picking a model tier is like picking a kitchen knife.
analogies.takeaway: A paring knife is fast and cheap for small jobs. A chef knife is the daily default. A cleaver is overkill until you need to break down a roast.

analogies.setup: Reasoning vs non-reasoning is like driving vs taking a long way with a map.
analogies.takeaway: Driving fast is fine when the route is obvious. The detour with a map is slower but stops you missing the exit.

misconceptions.truth: On most product tasks the mid tier wins on cost-quality. Use the flagship only when you measured a real gap.

misconceptions.myth: Benchmark leaderboards tell you which model to ship.
misconceptions.truth: Public benchmarks leak into training data and rarely match your eval. Run your own evals on your own tasks.

misconceptions.myth: A bigger context window solves retrieval.
misconceptions.truth: Models miss details in the middle of long contexts. Retrieval and chunking still help, even at 1M tokens.

glossary.definition: A chunk of text the model reads or writes. Roughly four characters of English.
The maximum tokens the model can see in one call, prompt plus answer.
A model trained to plan and verify steps before answering. Slower, more accurate on hard tasks.
A model that takes more than one input type, like text and images, or text and audio.
The most capable model in a family. Highest cost, latency, and quality ceiling.
A standardized test used to compare models. Useful for ranking, weak for product decisions.
Pulling only the relevant chunks of a document into the prompt instead of stuffing everything.

practicalTakeaways: Default to the mid tier; promote to flagship only after a real eval shows a gap.
Cache prompts and structured outputs; cost scales with what you re-send.
Pair retrieval with long context; do not throw whole codebases at the model.
Run a small private eval before you swap providers; benchmarks lie about your tasks.
Re-price every quarter; per-token cost has dropped about an order of magnitude in two years.

uncertaintyNotes: Specific model names and prices change monthly; this fixture intentionally avoids naming exact versions.
Context-window numbers vary by provider and tier; treat the 1M figure as illustrative, not a contract.
